stimulus: The Japanese aesthetic concept of wabi-sabi—an appreciation for impermanence, imperfection, and the patina of age—has long shaped traditional crafts such as tea-ware and architecture. Scholar Naomi Tagawa cautions that translating wabi-sabi simply as “rustic beauty” in English-language texts is misleading: it reduces the concept to a purely visual style and obscures the ethical and contemplative dimensions of wabi-sabi as a philosophy of how to live with material things over time.
stem: Which statement, if true, would most directly support Tagawa’s claim?

answerOptions.content: Classical Japanese tea-ceremony manuals describe wabi-sabi not only as a quality of objects but as a disciplined attitude of the host toward guests, care, and the passage of time.
The English phrase “rustic beauty” first appears in mid-twentieth-century translations of Japanese essays on tea-ware.
Many contemporary museum exhibitions use the phrase “rustic beauty” in wall texts because curators consider it accessible to broad audiences.
Modern Japanese ceramics that exhibit asymmetry and visible cracks are often described as wabi-sabi by Japanese critics and collectors alike.

rationale: Choice A is the best answer because it directly demonstrates that wabi-sabi in classical sources extends beyond appearance to include ethical and contemplative aspects—exactly what Tagawa says is obscured by “rustic beauty.” Choice B is about translation history, not whether the concept exceeds appearance. Choice C describes curatorial practice, not the concept’s meaning. Choice D affirms only the visual sense of wabi-sabi, which is the reduced sense Tagawa criticizes.
